summary: Gruber pushes back on Elon Musk's vision of turning Twitter into an 'everything app' modeled after China's WeChat. He argues that WeChat's dominance is a product of China's authoritarian system, not a model to emulate, and that cramming disparate features into a single app produces a worse experience than purpose-built apps on a capable OS. He points out that Facebook and Amazon already failed at similar expansionist strategies a decade ago. Gruber concludes that what people actually need isn't an everything app but an everything device — the smartphone — running small, focused apps.
key_points: WeChat is the only 'everything app' anyone can cite, and its dominance is inseparable from China's authoritarian regime — it benefits the CCP, not users.
Building an everything app means putting an entire OS inside an app, and history shows sprawling monolithic apps never deliver a coherent user experience.
Twitter is already more confusing than the platforms it would need to subsume — it needs to do less, not more.
Facebook and Amazon both tried expansionist platform plays with their own phones and failed badly despite being far more capable companies than Twitter.
The smartphone is already the 'everything device,' replacing 13 of 15 gadgets from a 1991 Radio Shack ad — the answer is focused apps on a capable platform, not one app to rule them all.
money_quotes: It's no coincidence at all that WeChat is the only 'everything app' anyone can cite, and it comes from China, an authoritarian regime.
It doesn't benefit users that WeChat dominates all aspects of digital life — it benefits the authoritarian Chinese Communist Party.
What you're talking about is putting an entire OS inside an app.
History shows that sprawling monolithic apps are never a good experience in practice.
Who looks at Twitter of all things and says 'I'd like to see this expand in scope such that a lot more, if not all, of my digital life can be here'?
Twitter, today, is already more confusing and less cohesive than the iOS or Android systems themselves.
What Twitter needs is to do less, certainly not more.
What we need isn't an everything app. It's an everything device, with small focused apps for features.
key_insight: The 'everything app' concept is a product of authoritarianism, not good design — the smartphone already is the everything device, and focused apps on a capable OS will always beat a monolithic super-app.
